docs.title: Welcome to the Environmental Change and Human Outcomes (ECHO) Lab!
What is the ECHOLab?
docs.text: The EchoLab, now part of the Doerr School of Sustainability, is a multidisciplinary team spanning from social and earth scientists, and epidemiologists with the common goal of measuring and understanding the effects of environmental change on present and future human systems.

docs.title: What is in here?
docs.text:
This is a compilation of different advice from past and current members of the team. We aspire to have a live-document that unifies the knowledge available in disparate places across the web, such as computation resources, graduate student resources or RAs benefits and opportunities. We also intent to have an on-boarding document that soft-lands the new members of our team.

This document is crowd-sourced and it needs your help to keep it up-to-date and in good shape for others to use. Once you become a tenured ECHOer, you need to share your new tricks here to others. See the contributing guidelines.

docs.title: How to contribute to this document?
docs.text: This website is hosted on Github pages, created with mkdocs, and is rendered on Github via Github Actions. Any commit done on the main branch will trigger the rendering of this webpage, updating its content on real-time. To make changes to this documents, is important to understand the structure of mkdocs.

docs.title: Documentation stucture
docs.text:
The main configuration file for mkdocs is mkdocs.yml always at the root of the repo folder. All details related to general configuration, such as the navigation tree (nav) or the configuration of new plugins. To create a new section, you just need to create a markdown document (the extension is .md) and put it in the right category. For instance, we can create a new section new_doc under General Advice by changing our config file:

mkdocs.yml
nav:
  - Home: index.md
  - General Advice: 
    - 'new_doc.md'

YAML is tricky

Remember YAML documents are indented, so keep consistency between each of the levels of the document. Not keeping this consistency can break the correct flow of the pages.

Not every change in the documents involve modifying the mkdocs.yml. If you only want to change an existing section, you can just easily just change the document in the docs/ folder. You can follow the Markdown documentation to modify the documents. Additionaly, you can check out some of the additional Python-flavored Markdown plugins from the Material theme docs.

docs.title: Local rendering
docs.text:
Github actions are limited. Despite using a small instance and a lightweight configuration, we only have a limited number of runs available. Thus, to avoid running out of computing hours, you can render the document locally and see live changes on your own browser. You will need python>=3.8 and hopefully a virtual environment.

Note

If you are new to Python. You can try the Miniconda distribution. Is a simple way to get a fast running Python environment without much configuration. Check out the latest version here and use it to create a virtual environment (some tips here). For more advance users, there is also a pyenv alternative.

Once you have your prefered Python version you can just:

condapyenv 
conda env create --file environment.yml --force
conda activate mkdocs_echolab

pyenv virtualenv 3.8 mkdocs_echolab
pyenv local mkdocs_echolab
pip install -r requirements.txt

Now, make sure you are in the root of the repo folder and render the document in your localhost by using: mkdocs serve. Notice that any change you make to this documents will be automatically made on the web browser.

docs.title: Password protected content 🔒
docs.text:
This webpage is open to the public. Nonetheless, we can password-protect a page, or a complete section using the many plugins in mkdocs. To add a password-protected document, you just need to change the header of the Markdown file you want to change. Python Markdown allows YAML header configurations to each md file, thus you need to change the key in password. For example: 

---
author: topcat
date: %today
password: this_is_a_safe_password
---
# A password protected document

docs.title: Contribution Etiquette
docs.text:
Before submitting any change, we go by the mantra: "never commit to the main branch". Sometimes 💩 happens, and that why Oh Shit Git exists, but in principle you do not commit to the main branch. When working in this document the rules of engagement are defined by branching and PR submission. To do this, simply create a new branch: git checkout <name of new branch> and make all changes there. After pushing all changes to Github, you can create a Pull Request in the repository.

Once a PR is discussed and approved by other member of the lab, you can accept the change and merge any changes into the document.

Warning

Be careful with merge conflicts. Is possible that some merge conflicts might arise if there are changes in conflicting lines. You can always brute force the merge by using the --force flag, or just by forcing it on the PR. This might be the right call 95% of the times, be sure of not be the 5%.

docs.title: Hot-fixes and easy changes
docs.text:
If you are modifying a single file, or just solving small text changes, you can use the Github editor to modify the file. Just go to the .md file you want to modity and click the ✏️: button (you can also press the E key). Once you make your changes, be sure of select the option Create a new branch for this commit and start a pull request.

If you consider your change can be directly commited to main, you are wrong. Leave it as a PR and find your nearest ECHOer to have a conversation about your change, and then both merge the PR! We ❤️ teamwork.

docs.title: Accounts and Access
docs.text:
• [ ] ECHOLab GitHub organization

    • Create a GitHub account if you do not already have one.
    • Provide your GitHub username to Sam.
    • Sam will add you to the ECHOLab GitHub organization.
    • GitHub organization: https://github.com/echolab-stanford

  • [ ] Lab shared calendar

    • Provide the Google account you would like to use for the ECHOLab shared calendar.
    • Sam will add you to the calendar.

  • [ ] Stanford FSE Slack workspace

    • Provide your Stanford email address.
    • Sam will add you to the Slack workspace.

  • [ ] Lab Dropbox account

    • Dropbox being phased out of lab activities but still useful for some applications.
    • To be added, provide the email address associated with your Dropbox account.
    • Sam will add you to the shared Dropbox workspace.

  • [ ] Sherlock and Oak access

    • Oak: Provide your Stanford email address. Sam will add you to Oak/mburke to get access to the lab's shared data.
  • [ ] Sherlock and Oak access
    • Oak: Provide your Stanford email address. Sam will add you to Oak/mburke to get access to the lab's shared data.
    • Sherlock: Request access through Stanford Research Computing. Sam or other lab members can help direct you to the appropriate contacts.
    • You will send an email requesting access and CC Marshall.
    • Stanford Research Computing will request Marshall's approval.
    • Marshall will approve the request and your account will be created.

docs.title: Lab Directory
docs.text:
• [ ] Lab website profile
    • Send a headshot to Marshall.
    • Provide any additional information needed for your profile page.

docs.title: ECHOLab Shared Calendar
docs.text:
Most lab members use a personal Google account for the ECHOLab shared calendar. This is generally the simplest option because all lab members have permission to edit the calendar, create events, and update meeting details.

To gain access, provide Sam with the Google account you would like to use for the calendar.

docs.title: Outlook / Stanford Calendar Integration
docs.text:
Some lab members primarily use their Stanford Outlook calendar to manage meetings and availability. In this case, you can subscribe to the ECHOLab Google Calendar so that ECHOLab events appear alongside your Stanford calendar events.

Notes: - The Outlook version of the calendar is read-only. - Calendar updates are not immediate and may take several hours to appear. - If you need to create or edit ECHOLab events, you should continue to use the Google Calendar directly.

Setup instructions:

  1. Obtain the ECHOLab calendar subscription URL from Sam.
  2. In Outlook Calendar, click Add Calendar.
  3. Select Subscribe from Web.
  4. Paste the calendar subscription URL.
  5. Choose a name and folder location for the calendar.
  6. Save the subscription.

docs.title: New Employee Onboarding Checklist
docs.text: This checklist covers Stanford-specific onboarding tasks for newly hired ECHOLab employees, including campus access and office setup.

docs.title: Campus and Office Setup
docs.text:
• [ ] Stanford ID card

    • Obtain a Stanford ID card during orientation or through the Stanford ID Card Office.

  • [ ] Building access

    • Activate your Stanford ID card for after-hours access to the building.
    • The building manager can assist with access requests.

  • [ ] Office key

    • Obtain a key for your shared office (Y2E2 362).
    • Sonal can assist with this process.

docs.title: Research Resources
docs.text:
This section describes common research workflows and shared resources used within ECHOLab.

The goal is not to prescribe a single workflow, but to make it easier for researchers to find data, understand projects, access computing resources, and collaborate effectively.

A key objective is that a new collaborator should be able to get up to speed on a project quickly through project documentation and know where to find the resources needed to understand, reproduce, and extend prior work.

docs.title: Research Workflow
docs.text:
Research projects involve many stages, from idea generation through publication and dissemination. This section is intended to help researchers identify resources that may be useful at different points in the research process.

The workflow below is not intended to be prescriptive. Projects rarely follow a linear path, and different researchers work in different ways. Instead, think of this page as a guide to finding relevant resources based on where you are in a project.

docs.title: 1. Research Idea Generation
docs.text:
Identifying research questions, finding gaps in the literature, evaluating novelty, and developing project ideas.

  • Geneating Research Ideas

docs.title: 2. Grant Writing and Project Development
docs.text:
Developing project concepts, preparing grant proposals, building collaborations, and translating ideas into funded research.

(Page under development.)

docs.title: 3. Project Setup and Organization
docs.text:
Creating a new project, selecting a repository structure, organizing files, establishing documentation, and planning for reproducibility.

Resources:

  • Project Documentation
  • Project Documentation Template
  • Project Communication Guidelines

docs.title: 4. Finding and Managing Data and Code
docs.text:
Identifying relevant datasets, using shared lab resources, documenting data sources, and promoting data reuse.

Resources:

  • Shared Data Catalog
  • Managing Shared Data
  • Oak Storage
  • Shared Code Resources

docs.title: 5. Project Analysis and Code Development
docs.text:
Organizing code, developing workflows, collaborating with others, and maintaining reproducible analyses.

Resources:

  • Working with Sherlock
  • Working with Oak

docs.title: 6. Figure Design and Data Visualization
docs.text:
Communicating results effectively through figures, visual summaries, and graphical storytelling.

Resources:

  • Figure Design

docs.title: 7. Scientific Writing
docs.text:
Structuring papers, communicating clearly, responding to reviewers, and developing a writing style.

Resources:

  • Scientific Writing (coming soon)

docs.title: 8. Publication and Dissemination
docs.text:
Selecting journals, navigating submission and revision, preparing press materials, and communicating research findings to broader audiences.

Resources:

  • Submitting Your Paper
  • Speaking with the Media About Your Research

docs.title: 9. Reproducibility and Replication
docs.text:
Ensuring that future collaborators can understand, reproduce, and extend a project.

Resources:

  • Pre-Publication Internal Replication
  • Post-Publication External Replication

Future topics:

  • Documentation Review
  • Project Archiving
  • Research Handoffs

docs.title: Starting a New Project
docs.text:
• Project Documentation
  • Project Documentation Template

docs.title: Looking for Data
docs.text:
• Shared Data Catalog
  • Managing Shared Data

docs.title: Accessing Oak
docs.text:
• Oak Storage
  • Working with Oak

docs.title: Using Sherlock
docs.text: • Working with Sherlock

docs.title: Preparing for Replication

• Pre-Publication Internal Replication
  • Post-Publication External Replication

docs.title: Research Infrastructure
docs.text: Resource Purpose GitHub Project documentation, code, and collaboration Oak Shared data storage Sherlock High-performance computing Overleaf Manuscript development Notion Project management Slack Communication

docs.title: Guiding Principle
docs.text:
Standardize interfaces, not workflows.

Researchers should have flexibility in how they conduct analyses and organize day-to-day work. However, projects should provide sufficient documentation and structure that future collaborators can understand project goals, locate resources, and contribute effectively.

The broader goal is to make projects, data, decisions, and workflows easy for future collaborators to discover, understand, reproduce, and extend.

docs.title: Communication and Collaboration
docs.text: Effective collaboration depends on making information easy to find, understand, and share. The goal of ECHOLab's communication practices is not to prescribe a single communication style, but to ensure that important information remains accessible to current and future collaborators.

docs.title: Communication Channels
docs.text:
ECHOLab uses a variety of communication tools for different purposes:

Tool Typical Use Slack Day-to-day communication and project discussions Email Communication outside the lab and formal correspondence GitHub Code review, project documentation, and technical discussions Notion Project planning, meeting notes, and task tracking Overleaf Manuscript development and collaboration

Slack is generally the preferred platform for routine communication within the lab.

docs.title: Public by Default
docs.text:
Direct messages are appropriate for quick questions, scheduling, and other minor communications.

However, substantive project discussions, decisions, and information that may be useful to future collaborators should generally occur in project channels or be documented elsewhere.

Future collaborators should be able to understand the history of a project without relying on access to private conversations.

docs.title: Project Channels
docs.text:
Active projects are encouraged to maintain dedicated Slack channels when practical.

Project channels provide a shared location for:

  • Project discussions
  • Announcements and updates
  • Questions and troubleshooting
  • Sharing resources and references
  • Coordination among collaborators

When possible, project communication resources should be linked from the project's GitHub repository.

docs.title: Document Important Decisions
docs.text:
Important project decisions should be documented in a location that collaborators can easily discover and reference.

Examples may include:

  • Project README files
  • Notion pages
  • Meeting notes
  • GitHub issues or pull requests
  • Project Slack channels

The specific location is less important than ensuring that information remains accessible to collaborators.

docs.title: Reduce Single Points of Failure
docs.text:
Information that is important to a project should not exist solely in one person's memory, email inbox, or direct messages.

Whenever practical, project knowledge should be documented in locations that remain available to collaborators over time.

Doing so reduces onboarding costs, improves continuity as researchers join and leave the lab, and helps ensure that projects remain understandable and reproducible.

docs.title: Meeting Notes
docs.text:
Meeting notes are encouraged for recurring project meetings and collaborative workstreams.

Notes can serve as a useful record of:

  • Decisions made
  • Open questions
  • Action items
  • Relevant resources and references

Meeting notes should be stored in a location accessible to project collaborators whenever practical.

docs.title: Guiding Principle
docs.text:
Make important information discoverable.

The objective is not to document everything. Rather, it is to ensure that information that is important to a project's success can be located, understood, and used by future collaborators.

docs.title: Computation at Stanford
docs.text:
Stanford has several places for computation! All students (undergrad and grad) have access to Farmshare, a small cluster with a queue and with very limited resources. Additionally, our group has access to Sherlock, the main computing cluster and a very cool and powerful tool when used responsibly.

We do not own any nodes at Sherlock, that means that we have to use a queue system and stay in line until computing resources are free to run our jobs. Sherlock's line is shared with all the university users, but as part of the School of Sustainability we have one of the largest partitions in Sherlock that is only used by researchers within the school. This allows our jobs to stay less time in the queue as the number of users is just a subset of all of Sherlock's users.

Note: If you do not yet have access to Sherlcok you need to request access through Stanford Research Computing.

docs.title: What is a cluster?
docs.text:
A computer cluster is in rough terms a set of shared computers that are connected to each other while sharing memory and processing power. Thus, we can run jobs a (jobs is the name we give to running a script on the cluster) across nodes or within each of the nodes (nodes are BIG computers). All nodes are coordinated by a scheduler, in Sherlock case, we use SLURM, a very flexible scheduler used by most of the big clusters around the world, including the beautiful Cheyenne, the NCAR super-computer.

Sherlock is a shared resource

Every time you use Sherlock, SLURM will penalize you and the whole lab! SLURM relies on the FairShare, an algorithm that decides what's the fair use of computation resources for each user and group. Therefore, everytime you run a job be sure of asking the resources you really need (i.e. number of hours, cores, and memory).

In general, clusters have three types of nodes:

  • Login: Nodes meant to welcome the user and meant for anything but computation. They're named usually: sh02-lnx, where x is the login node identity number.
  • Computing: This nodes will run jobs. Any code always runs in these nodes. These nodes are named usually: sh03-09n59, where 09n59 is a specific node in the whole cluster.
  • Storage: Rarely used, but you can access to nodes that are meant just for data transmission. This can be either moving a lot of data out or to Sherlock.

In this document we will focus on the SERC partition. You can check the partition characteristics by using the sh_part command. If you have access to other partitions or your PI (in case you have a second PI) has nodes, you will see the information for those nodes there too.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
Having all the software binaries loaded into the $PATH1 can lead to chaos. For this reason, Sherlock uses the Modules system. Loading modules is part of the life in the cluster and is essential not only for running code, but also to compile packages (i.e. installing R's sf needs you to load several modules. Same is true for many Python libraries).

Sherlock has some amazing documentation on Modules, so we won't cover that here. Just some basics for our lab:

PythonROther stuff 
module load python/3.9.0

module load R/4.2.0

module spider <other stuff to look for>

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
We have plenty of storage space in Sherlock! For job running, is good to use fast file systems, like the $SCRATCH space. For permanent data storage and files that will be concurrently used, the $OAK and $GROUP_HOME are good alternatives. As all good things in the cluster, remember that $OAK is a shared space within the SDSS, so think of other users before wanting to store those 100 Tb of CMIP data you probably do not need.

Storage Path Total space Time Limit $OAK /oak/stanford/schools/ees/mburke 1 Pb [100 Tb/group] \(\infty\) $GROUP_HOME /home/groups/mburke 100 Tb \(\infty\) $GROUP_SCRATCH /scratch/groups/mburke 100 Tb 90 days

SCRATCH is deleted every 90 days!

Be careful with the scratch space! Be sure to remove all the files you need after you run a job. Files in this storage are not backed-up, so there will be no way to recover.

docs.title: Oak
docs.text:
Oak is shared with all the SDSS. You can create folders in the root of the server (the path in the table above) if you need to share files with people across the school (i.e. ESS, EPS, or rarely, E-IPER). For lab-wide data, we use the mburke directory. We do not have any particular nomenclature for our filesystem, but try to be use sensible and informative names:

  • 🚫 $OAK/mburke/results
  • ✅ $OAK/mburke/<project_name>
  • ✅ $OAK/mburke/ERA_5_1979_2023

To avoid permission and possible file corruption avoid cloning repos directly into $OAK, use your $HOME for that and store your data in any of the storages described above

docs.title: Running jobs
docs.text: There are different ways of running jobs in Sherlock. Compared with other clusters, Sherlock does not have any computing quotas, rather you can run as many jobs you want until the scheduler stop giving giving you jobs. In general, we have two ways of running jobs:

docs.title: In-line
docs.text:
SLURM allow us to request nodes and run jobs directly into them! We have several use cases for this request:

  • Testing a pipeline: I have built a Python script hello.py and want to test it before submitting a larger job.
  • Running small tests: I have a GPU job and I want to test my Python environment is correctly compiling the right CUDA version. I cannot test this is a login node.
  • Running a Jupyter Notebook: I want to run a quick jupyter notebook without having to deal with a job submission.

In-line jobs are very useful, especially for testing and debugging, but remember that is always better to have pipelines that are less interactive and that can run unsupervised. To run in-line jobs, you have to use the sdev command:

Example

sdev commands: 

$ sdev -h
sdev: start an interactive shell on a compute node.

Usage: sdev [OPTIONS]
    Optional arguments:
        -c      number of CPU cores to request (OpenMP/pthreads, default: 1)
        -g      number of GPUs to request (default: none)
        -n      number of tasks to request (MPI ranks, default: 1)
        -N      number of nodes to request (default: 1)
        -m      memory amount to request (default: 4GB)
        -p      partition to run the job in (default: dev)
        -t      time limit (default: 01:00:00)
        -r      allocate resources from the named reservation (default: none)
        -J      job name (default: sdev)
        -q      quality of service to request for the job (default: normal)

    Note: the default partition only allows for limited amount of resources.
    If you need more, your job will be rejected unless you specify an
    alternative partition with -p.

You can request a job with 1 node, 16 cores and 32 Gb of RAM in the SERC partition (just like the average MacBookPro) for 30 minutes using the following:

sdev -n 1 -c 16 -m 16G -p serc -t 00:30:00 --pty bash

Once you run this job, you will get assigned a job number and when the resources are allocated you will have access to that computing node terminal. Inside the computing node you have access to any folder or file stored in Sherlock and you can run any binary using the module command.

docs.title: Batch job
docs.text:
Compared to the in-line job, we won't have any access to the computing node terminal. The job will run completely unsupervised and if it fails then the job will stop executing and will be cancelled and two files will be written: an output file (usually with an .out extension), and an error file with all the error contents (with an .err extension)

Job exampleAnother exampleSubmit your job 
#!/usr/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test_job
#SBATCH --output=test_job.out
#SBATCH --error=test_job.err
#SBATCH --time=00:30:00
#SBATCH -p serc
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --mem 16GB
#SBATCH -c 4
#SBATCH --mail-user=ihigueme@stanford.edu
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL

sleep 10
echo "Hello from the ${HOST}"

#!/usr/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test_job
#SBATCH --output=test_job.out
#SBATCH --error=test_job.err
#SBATCH --time=00:30:00
#SBATCH -p gpu
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --mem 16GB
#SBATCH --gpu 1
#SBATCH -c 4
#SBATCH --mail-user=ihigueme@stanford.edu
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL

sleep 10
echo "Hello from the ${HOST} with a GPU"

$ sbatch test_job.sbatch
Submitted batch job 22312518 # This is your job id

As you can see, there is some overlap between the options we used in sdev and the ones we use in our batch job file. Any SLURM command should be prepended by the #SBATCH flag, this is telling the computer that is a command for the scheduler and not a local variable. Let's go over some of the commands use above:

--job-name Name for the job. Is useful for traceability when calling squeue or just to check the history of commands ran in the past. --output and --error Name for output and error files. Usually saved in the same directory where the batch file is located. You can specify another path. If left empty, the default will be <job-name><job-id>.err --time Time we are asking the scheduler to keep the job alive. Once the time is done, the job will be stopped. Plan accordignly and test before running a long job. Sherlock has limits, so jobs shouldn't last more than 7 days. -p Paritition. The default is using the main queue, but there are many available (list them using the sh_part command). Use dev if you want to test, and serc when you're ready to send jobs. --nodes Number of nodes to request. Usually this is set to 1 because it fits most of the vanilla parallelization schemes in Python and R. Use more than 1 node if, and only if, you're using OpenMPI powered code (i.e. C++ processes and sometimes Python's Dask). If using more than one node, many new options are open: --ntasks-per-node, and --cpus-per-task. Read more on the SLURM documentation. --mem and --cpu These two options set how much memory and cores you need. Remember that as a thumb of rule, these two go together. The more cores you use in parallelization, the more memory you will need since cores are sharing data over RAM. serc nodes have between 24 and 128 cores, and between 191G and 1024G of RAM memory. Your job should not exceed any of these limits, and usually not be even close to that maximum. Adding more memory and more cores is often not a good solution to slow code (check out the piece about the Amdahl's Rule here). --mail-user and --mail-type This is nice-to-have in your job, especially if you're in a long queue. This command will send an email to the email under mail-user once the job started to run, finished to run, or just failed. Usually has nice data on usage that you can use to set your next job.

docs.title: Some tricks and common issues in Sherlock
Do ssh like a pro
docs.text: Tired of doing ssh <user>@sherlock.stanford.edu and being thrown into whatever login node they want? Want to set up an SSH-tunnel from the get-go with a pre-determined port (v.gr. this is very useful for running Jupyter notebooks in Sherlock nodes)? The SSH config is what you need. The Sherlock documentation has some details on this.

docs.title: tmux is your best friend
docs.text:
Back in the day, life in the terminal was the common place. Unix has developed very good window managers that allow you to have horizontal and vertical splits, and several windows within a unique ssh connection. More importantly, these managers also allow you to run a process and let it run even if you disconnect from Sherlock. There are two main programs that serve this functions: screen and tmux. The later is better maintained, and is also my favorite ❤️

Tmux has a good starter guide, and is extremely configurable using its dotfile: ~/.tmux.conf. I know, what the heck are dotfiles? Check out more about them here.

docs.title: Building bash scripts
docs.text:
An essential tool of the terminal is to learn how to run basic bash files. These files are very flexible and allow to build command-line interfaces:

A simple bash file: hello.shA bash file with commands 
#!/bin/bash
echo "Hello world!"

#!/bin/bash
# Print whatever argument we put on the command
$ARG=$1
echo "{$ARG}"

Bash is incredibly flexible and powerful. These scripts are particularly useful when creating batch jobs or just simplifying how we run code. More details on how to write and run these are here.

docs.title: My Windows machine is not compatible with X, Y or Z
docs.text: Truly unfortunate. You have a 2K one-time grant as a SDSS student. Change computers, many good Linux Machines are also available with better specs than a MacBook Pro at a similar price range. If you want to stay with Windows, update to the Windows 10/11 edition and install the Ubuntu terminal.

docs.title: I don't have access to $OAK, what should I do?
docs.text: Checkout the SERC OAK documentation

docs.title: My job is stuck in (Priority)
docs.text: Life is hard. Just wait

docs.title: I need some help with my code
docs.text: Getting help for Sherlock is quite easy. You can either ask for help to people in the lab, or you can also use the #sherlock-users channel on Slack where Kilian Cavalotti or any of the cluster's users are happy to help you with any problem. You can also check many of the great resources put together by the SDSS-CFS

docs.title: A few remarks on environment reproducibility
Python
docs.text:
Sherlock modules do not include any distribution of Anaconda. If you want to install Conda2 or Mamba, you should install it on your $HOME directory. Nonetheless, these Python distributions are very bloated and do not play well with Sherlock. An alternative to this is to load the python/3.9.0 module, and then use vritualenv.

Load modules and install virtualenvCreate environmentActivate environment 
module load python/3.9.0
# pip will install libraries locally in the $HOME
pip install virtualenv 

virtualenv -p python $SCRATCH/test_env

source $SCRATCH/test_env/bin/activate

# Test that I am using my environment python
# This should print the path to the environment
which python

# Once you check is the right path, you can install some libraries
pip install -r requirements.txt

Notice that I am creating my environment in the $SCRATCH space, this is due to space limitations in the $HOME. Sherlock only allows 15 Gb in your home directory, thus you should avoid to put environment or big files on it. Once you have reached your quota, Sherlock will stop writing files and this can lead to data corruption.

You do not need to use $SCRATCH, you can use either $GROUP_HOME or $GROUP_SCRATCH. Be aware you cannot share environments with people across the group, this because of permissions. A solution to this is always keeping a requirements.txt updated so people can reproduce your Python environment. A more advanced alternative is to use Singularity, a containerization module created specifically for HPC systems and developed by Stanford.

docs.title: A note on Conda/Mamba on Sherlock
docs.text:
By default, conda will install environments in your $HOME directory. Conda does not only install Python libraries, but also many binaries that it needs to compile package installation, this includes its own version of gcc and its own C++ libraries. Be aware that many of these are not optimized to Sherlock's hardware, and you should avoid these if you are looking for max performance (i.e. Sherlock has it's own compilation of PyTorch just to play nice with its GPUs).

A solution to Conda's storage problem is to change your ~/.condarc file and use the following (remember to change your user name in the file below)

~/.condarc 
always_yes: true
envs_dirs:
  - /scratch/users/{your user name}/conda_envs

channels:
  - conda-forge
  - defaults
channel_priority: strict

More details in how to manage virtualenv environments is here.

docs.title: Some reading material
docs.text:
• Ten Simple Rules for Success with HPC - Imperial College

  1. This is the environment that contains the path to the executables we want to run. Go ahead and run echo $PATH to see where is the computer looking for executable binaries. ↩

  2. I do recommend to use Mamba over Conda, is not only faster, but also way more reliable and based on the conda-forge channel. ↩

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
ECHOLab projects rely on a combination of shared datasets, project-specific datasets, and derived data products. We want to ensure that data resources remain discoverable, well-organized, and accessible to future researchers while minimizing unnecessary duplication.

This page describes general principles for managing shared data resources. Specific organizational structures and practices may evolve over time.

docs.title: Maintain Shared Resources
docs.text:
Datasets that are used across multiple projects should be maintained as shared resources whenever possible.

Examples include:

  • ERA5
  • DHS
  • WorldClim
  • CMIP6
  • PurpleAir

docs.title: Minimize Duplication
docs.text:
Researchers should look for existing shared resources before creating new copies of large datasets.

The Shared Data Catalog can help researchers identify existing datasets and data products.

docs.title: Preserve Canonical Copies
docs.text:
Shared datasets should have a clearly identified canonical location.

Researchers should avoid modifying shared raw datasets directly. Other projects may rely on those data, and changes can make it difficult to reproduce prior analyses.

When updates are needed, new data should generally be added rather than replacing existing files.

When multiple versions of a dataset exist, they should be clearly versioned and retained whenever feasible. Preserving older versions helps ensure that previous projects remain reproducible and allows researchers to understand which version of a dataset was used in a particular analysis.

In general, raw data should be treated as immutable. Data cleaning, processing, and analysis should occur in project-specific workflows rather than by modifying shared raw datasets.

docs.title: Separate Shared Data from Project Data
docs.text:
Not all data belong in shared storage.

In general:

  • Shared datasets support multiple projects.
  • Project data are specific to a particular project.
  • Derived products may fall into either category depending on anticipated reuse.

docs.title: Support Reproducibility
docs.text:
Project workflows should make it possible to identify:

  • Which datasets were used
  • Where they were stored
  • How they were processed

Good project documentation can substantially reduce the effort required to prepare replication materials.

docs.title: Future Topics
docs.text:
The following topics are still under discussion:

  • Shared dataset stewardship
  • Dataset versioning
  • Canonical storage locations
  • Archive policies
  • Data lifecycle management
  • Project offboarding

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
ECHOLab maintains a number of shared datasets and data products that are used across multiple projects. This page serves as a directory of commonly used data resources and where they can be found.

The goal of this page is to help researchers discover existing data resources before creating new copies or repeating data acquisition and processing work.

This page is intended to provide a high-level inventory rather than detailed documentation. Individual datasets may have separate documentation pages describing acquisition, processing, versioning, and usage considerations.

For information on data governance, storage policies, and working with Oak, see the related documentation pages.

Note: Location refers to the current location of the dataset and not necessarily its long-term canonical location. As ECHOLab continues migrating shared resources to Oak, dataset locations may change.

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Oak Storage
  • Working with Oak
  • Data Governance
  • Shared Pipelines

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Figures are one of the most important tools for communicating research. A good figure helps readers quickly understand an idea, result, or pattern that might otherwise require substantial text to explain.

Some useful goals when designing figures:

  • Readers should be able to understand the main message without relying heavily on the caption.
  • A figure should communicate a clear idea, not simply display data.
  • Main results figures should be able to stand on their own and tell an important part of the paper's story.
  • Particularly successful introductory or contextual figures are often reused by others in presentations, lectures, policy briefs, and other communication materials.

There is rarely a single correct way to visualize information. The goal is not simply to display data, but to communicate clearly.

docs.title: Start with the Message
docs.text:
Before creating a figure, ask:

What is the main thing I want the reader to learn?

Different figures serve different purposes, including:

  • Summarizing data
  • Presenting main results
  • Exploring mechanisms or heterogeneity
  • Providing context
  • Building confidence in a result
  • Explaining a concept or workflow

A figure should have a clear purpose and a clear takeaway.

docs.title: Prioritize Clarity
docs.text:
Readers should be able to understand the main point of a figure quickly.

Consider:

  • Clear labels and titles
  • Readable text
  • Logical panel organization
  • Direct annotations when helpful
  • Consistent visual encodings

Whenever possible, avoid making readers search for important information.

docs.title: Keep Things Simple
docs.text:
Every visual element should have a purpose.

When reviewing a figure, ask:

If I removed this, would the figure become harder to understand?

If not, consider removing it.

Simple figures are often more effective than complicated ones.

docs.title: Choose the Right Visualization
docs.text:
Different questions call for different plot types. Do not default to a particular chart simply because it is familiar.

When looking for ideas, it is often helpful to draw inspiration from:

  • Scientific papers
  • Data journalism
  • Visualization galleries
  • Previous work from the lab

Many effective figures are adaptations of ideas used elsewhere.

docs.title: Be Consistent
docs.text:
Within a project, related figures should feel related.

Consider using:

  • Consistent color schemes
  • Similar visual styles
  • Common labeling conventions

Consistency makes projects easier to follow and reduces cognitive burden for readers.

docs.title: Be Honest
docs.text:
Figures should help readers understand the data, not exaggerate or obscure it.

Use scales, colors, labels, and visual encodings thoughtfully. Whenever practical, show the underlying data and provide enough context for readers to interpret results accurately.

docs.title: Iterate
docs.text:
Strong figures are rarely produced on the first attempt.

Experiment with different layouts, plot types, annotations, and color schemes. Small changes can substantially improve interpretability.

docs.title: Color Palettes and Design Resources
docs.text: The resources below can be useful starting points when looking for color palettes or figure design inspiration.

docs.title: Color Palettes
docs.text:
• MetBrewer – palettes inspired by artwork from the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
  • MoMAColors – palettes inspired by artwork from the Museum of Modern Art.
  • wesanderson – palettes inspired by Wes Anderson films.
  • PNWColors – palettes inspired by landscapes and imagery from the Pacific Northwest.

docs.title: Visualization Inspiration
docs.text:
• R Graph Gallery
  • The New York Times Graphics
  • The Economist Graphics

docs.title: Community Favorites
docs.text: This section is intentionally informal. If you discover a palette, visualization resource, paper, or figure that you find particularly useful, consider adding it here so others can benefit from it.

docs.title: Final Thought
docs.text:
A useful test is to imagine a future collaborator encountering the figure with little context.

Could they understand the main message without reading the caption?

For important figures, another useful question is:

Would someone else want to borrow this figure for the introduction to a presentation on the topic?

The best figures make ideas easy to discover, understand, communicate, and reuse.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Generating research ideas is often one of the least structured parts of the research process. While there is no formula for identifying important questions, it can be helpful to evaluate ideas systematically.

The goal is not simply to identify interesting topics, but to identify projects that are likely to succeed and have meaningful scientific or real-world impact.

docs.title: From Topics to Research Questions
docs.text:
Many research ideas begin as broad topics:

  • Climate change and migration
  • Air pollution and health
  • Wildfire smoke
  • Artificial intelligence and education

However, topics are not research questions.

A useful research question should be specific enough that it can be answered empirically.

For example:

Topic

Climate change and migration

Research question

How do droughts affect rural-to-urban migration in sub-Saharan Africa?

Testable hypothesis

Areas experiencing severe drought will experience increased out-migration relative to comparable areas not experiencing drought.

A useful exercise is to ask:

  • What exactly am I trying to learn?
  • What comparison is being made?
  • What evidence would support or refute the hypothesis?

If a question cannot be translated into a testable hypothesis, it may still be too broad.

docs.title: Four Dimensions of a Strong Research Question
Importance
docs.text:
Does answering the question matter?

  • Would it change how people think?
  • Would it influence policy, practice, or future research?
  • What decisions could it inform?

docs.title: Tractability
docs.text:
Can the question actually be answered?

  • Are suitable data available?
  • Is there a plausible empirical strategy?
  • Can the project be completed with available resources?

docs.title: Interest
docs.text:
Will people care about the answer?

  • Is the question intellectually engaging?
  • Will others find the answer informative or surprising?
  • Is there a compelling story to tell?

docs.title: Comparative Advantage
docs.text:
Why are we well positioned to answer this question?

  • Do we have access to unique data?
  • Do we have specialized expertise?
  • Do we have collaborators or resources that make the project feasible?

docs.title: Favor Answer-Invariant Questions
docs.text:
One useful principle is:

The best research questions remain interesting regardless of the answer.

A useful test is:

Would this project still be worth publishing if the main result were null?

Strong research questions reduce uncertainty rather than confirm expectations.

docs.title: Consider the End Product
docs.text:
Before investing substantial effort in a project, it can be useful to think about what a successful final paper would look like.

Questions to consider:

  • What is the main contribution?
  • Who would care about the results?
  • What decisions could the findings inform?
  • What journal or audience might be interested?

One useful exercise is to draft a hypothetical abstract for the completed paper. The goal is not to predict the results, but to clarify the project's framing, contribution, and potential impact.

docs.title: Final Questions
docs.text:
Before committing substantial effort to a project, it can be useful to ask:

  • Is the question clearly defined?
  • Can it be translated into a testable hypothesis?
  • Is it important?
  • Is it tractable?
  • Is it interesting?
  • Do we have a comparative advantage?
  • Would it still be interesting if the answer were null?
  • What decisions could the findings inform?

Projects that score highly across these dimensions are often the most rewarding to pursue.

docs.title: Speaking with the Media About Your Research
Overview
docs.text:
Media interviews are different from academic presentations. Reporters are often looking for clear explanations, memorable quotes, practical implications, and connections to ongoing public discussions.

The goal is not to simplify away the science. The goal is to communicate the science clearly and accurately.

docs.title: 1. Prepare Key Messages
docs.text:
Before an interview:

  • Prepare a one- or two-sentence summary of the paper.
  • Identify 3–5 key points you want to communicate.
  • Prepare a few memorable statistics that help convey the magnitude of the findings.
  • Review any accompanying commentary, editorial, or perspective article.

docs.title: 2. Think About the Quotes You Want Used
docs.text:
Only a small fraction of an interview typically appears in the final story.

  • Think about one or two statements you would be comfortable seeing quoted verbatim.
  • Reporters often look for concise explanations of why a result matters.
  • Clear, memorable statements are more likely to appear in coverage than long, heavily qualified responses.

docs.title: 3. Anticipate Questions Beyond the Paper
docs.text:
Many questions will not be about the specific analyses in your study.

Common examples include:

  • Why should people care?
  • What should people do with this information?
  • What policies should be implemented?
  • How does this compare with previous research?
  • What are the study's limitations?

docs.title: 4. Understand What Reporters Are Looking For
docs.text:
Researchers often focus on estimating effects or quantifying risks. Reporters and readers are often interested in a different question:

What should be done with this information?

For example:

  • A wildfire smoke study may lead readers to ask how they can protect themselves.
  • A climate-economics study may lead readers to ask what businesses or governments should do in response.

Much of environmental health research quantifies risk, but reporters and readers want to know how to respond to that risk. The answer may not come from the paper itself, but it is often the most important part of the interview.

docs.title: 5. Distinguish Between the Paper and the Evidence Base
docs.text:
Reporters will often ask questions that extend beyond the specific analyses in your paper.

A useful distinction is between:

  • What was directly examined in the paper.
  • What is supported by the broader body of evidence.

For example, a wildfire smoke study may not evaluate protective actions, but researchers can still discuss recommendations from public health agencies, clinicians, intervention studies, or other relevant evidence.

Useful phrases include:

  • "Our study found..."
  • "Based on the broader evidence, experts generally recommend..."

docs.title: 6. Prepare Useful Numbers
docs.text:
Specific statistics can make findings easier to understand and remember.

Consider preparing:

  • Key effect sizes.
  • Population impacts.
  • Comparisons that provide context.
  • Additional descriptive statistics that may help communicate results to non-specialists.

docs.title: 7. Bridge Back to Your Main Message
docs.text:
You do not need to answer every question exactly as it is asked.

It is often useful to briefly acknowledge a question and then transition back to the main point you want readers to remember.

Examples:

  • "That's an important question. What our study specifically found was..."
  • "While our study did not directly examine that issue, one important finding from our work is..."
  • "The main takeaway from our study is..."

Most readers will remember one or two messages from an interview. Repetition can be helpful.

docs.title: 8. Expect Simplification
docs.text:
News stories have much less space than scientific papers.

  • Important nuances may be omitted.
  • Findings may be simplified.
  • Results may be framed differently than they are in the paper.

The goal is to communicate clearly while minimizing opportunities for misunderstanding.

docs.title: 9. Learn From Early Coverage
docs.text:
After the first few interviews:

  • Identify questions reporters repeatedly ask.
  • Look for common misunderstandings.
  • Observe how different outlets frame the story.
  • Review expert commentary and outside perspectives.

Good reporters often introduce context and ideas that can help improve future interviews.

docs.title: 10. Remember That Media Ecosystems Are Connected
docs.text:
Many reporters read previous coverage when reporting on a study.

  • Early framing can influence later stories.
  • Misunderstandings can spread across outlets.
  • Clear communication early in the process can have outsized effects.

docs.title: Final Thought
docs.text:
A useful goal is to be able to explain your paper to an interested non-expert in one or two minutes.

If someone understands the main finding, why it matters, what decisions it might inform, and what its limitations are, the interview has likely been successful.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Oak is Stanford's high-capacity research storage system and serves as ECHOLab's primary platform for shared data storage.

Most lab datasets, project data, and shared resources should be stored on Oak whenever practical. Researchers use Oak to store large datasets, share data across projects, and access resources from both local machines and Sherlock.

Within ECHOLab, Oak is generally considered the canonical location for shared data resources. While researchers may maintain local working copies of datasets, Oak should be treated as the primary storage location for lab-mangaed data.

Note

Oak is ECHOLab's primary platform for shared data storage.

Researchers are encouraged to store shared datasets relaed to shared projects on Oak whenever practical rather than maintaining shared project-critical data solely on local machines.

docs.title: Why We Use Oak
docs.text:
Oak provides:

  • Large-scale storage for research data
  • Shared access across lab members
  • Integration with Sherlock
  • Backup and data management infrastructure
  • A central location for long-term project resources

Because many projects rely on shared datasets, Oak plays a critical role in collaboration, reproducibility, and continuity within the lab.

docs.title: Oak in the ECHOLab Infrastructure
docs.text:
ECHOLab relies on several core platforms:

Platform Purpose GitHub Code management and project hub Oak Shared data storage Sherlock Shared computing environment Overleaf Manuscript development Notion Project management and planning Slack Communication

Researchers should generally be able to locate project code, data, manuscripts, and documentation by starting from a project's GitHub repository and following links to the relevant resources.

docs.title: Oak and Sherlock
docs.text:
Oak and Sherlock serve different roles within the ECHOLab computing environment.

  • Oak is primarily used for data storage.
  • Sherlock is primarily used for computation.

A common workflow is:

Raw data on Oak
    ↓
Processing on Sherlock
    ↓
Outputs written back to Oak
    ↓
Analysis performed locally or on Sherlock

Researchers should become familiar with both systems early in their time in the lab.

For detailed guidance on Sherlock, see the ECHOLab Computing Guide.

docs.title: Oak is the Canonical Source of Truth
docs.text:
Researchers may maintain local copies of datasets for performance, convenience, or offline work.

However:

  • Shared datasets should live on Oak.
  • Oak should be treated as the canonical source of truth.
  • Local copies should be viewed as working copies.
  • Important updates should be synchronized back to Oak.

The existence of a file on a researcher's laptop should not be assumed to imply that it has been shared with the rest of the lab.

docs.title: Local Mirrors Are Not Automatically Synchronized
docs.text:
Note that synchronization between Oak and local machines is generally an explicit action.

This means:

  • New files created on Oak do not automatically appear on your laptop.
  • New files created on your laptop do not automatically appear on Oak.
  • Researchers are responsible for running synchronization commands when appropriate.

Always verify what will be transferred before performing large synchronization operations.

docs.title: Connecting to Oak
docs.text:
It is sometimes convenient to access Oak directly from your local machines using SSHFS. Mounting Oak locally can make it easier to browse files, inspect datasets, and work with project resources without repeatedly copying data between systems.

Typical setup steps include:

  1. Configure SSH access to Oak.
  2. Create a local mount point (e.g., ~/oak).
  3. Mount Oak using SSHFS.
  4. Configure SSH keys to reduce repeated two-factor authentication prompts.
  5. Access Oak through Finder or the command line.

Researchers who regularly work with Oak may find it helpful to create shell aliases for mounting and unmounting Oak.

Example workflow:

Local Machine
      ↓
 Mount Oak with SSHFS
      ↓
 Access files through ~/oak
      ↓
 Synchronize data as needed with rsync

Oak can also be accessed directly through SSH and rsync without mounting the filesystem locally. This approach is often more reliable for large file transfers and automated workflows.

For current Stanford-specific setup instructions, see the official Oak documentation.

docs.title: Synchronization Best Practices
docs.text:
Researchers may maintain local working copies of datasets while storing shared resources on Oak.

When synchronizing files:

  • Use dry runs before large transfers whenever possible.
  • Verify that files are moving in the expected direction.
  • Be especially cautious when overwriting or deleting files.
  • Remember that synchronization is not automatic.
  • Confirm that important local work has been copied back to Oak.

In general, Oak should be treated as the authoritative copy of shared project data, while local copies should be treated as working copies.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Shared Oak directories often use a permission structure that distinguishes between contributors and administrators.

Researchers may have permission to:

  • Read files
  • Create files
  • Modify files
  • Extend datasets

while lacking permission to:

  • Move directories
  • Rename directories
  • Reorganize shared folder structures
  • Modify access controls

This behavior is intentional and helps protect shared resources from accidental disruption.

docs.title: Governance and Permissions
docs.text:
Whenever practical, governance policies should align with Oak permissions.

Examples:

Action Contributor Dataset Steward Add files ✓ ✓ Update files ✓ ✓ Update documentation ✓ ✓ Rename shared directories ✓ Move shared directories ✓ Modify permissions ✓

Researchers who need structural changes to shared resources should coordinate with the relevant dataset owner or steward.

TODO: Add guidance on ACLs, ownership, and troubleshooting permission errors.

docs.title: Shared Data Philosophy
docs.text:
Shared datasets are among ECHOLab's most valuable research assets.

The goal of ECHOLab's data management practices is to ensure that shared datasets are:

  • Easy to find
  • Easy to understand
  • Easy to extend
  • Easy to maintain
  • Easy to reproduce

A researcher should be able to locate a shared dataset, understand how it was created, determine how it has changed over time, and use it in their own work without relying on institutional knowledge from the original creator.

Whenever practical, shared datasets should include:

  • Documentation describing the data and its provenance
  • Information about how the data were obtained or generated
  • Changelogs documenting major updates
  • Links to relevant code repositories or processing pipelines
  • Identified maintainers or points of contact

The long-term objective is to ensure that shared data resources remain useful and maintainable even as researchers join and leave the lab.

docs.title: Shared vs Project-Specific Data
docs.text:
ECHOLab maintains both:

  • Shared datasets used across multiple projects.
  • Project-specific datasets maintained for individual projects.

These should be clearly distinguished whenever possible.

TODO: Document recommended directory structures and naming conventions.

docs.title: Dataset Stewardship
docs.text:
Shared datasets should have designated maintainers.

Maintainers are responsible for:

  • Documentation
  • Versioning
  • Permission management
  • Structural changes

Contributors may extend datasets and update contents, but major reorganizations of shared resources should be coordinated with dataset maintainers.

TODO: Develop dataset stewardship framework.

docs.title: Documentation Expectations
docs.text:
Shared datasets should include:

  • README documentation
  • Data dictionaries when practical
  • Changelogs describing major updates
  • Information on data provenance

Whenever data are added to a shared resource, documentation should be updated to record:

  • Date of update
  • Person making the update
  • Description of changes
  • Relevant code or data source information

TODO: Create standard dataset README template.

docs.title: Common ECHOLab Workflows
docs.text:
TODO

Potential examples:

  • Working locally with a mounted Oak drive
  • Synchronizing local mirrors with rsync
  • Processing large datasets on Sherlock
  • Maintaining shared datasets on Oak
  • Collaborating across multiple projects using shared data resources

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Working with Oak
  • Managing Shared Data
  • Official Oak Documentation

These resources describe the underlying storage system and institutional policies. The ECHOLab documentation focuses on lab-specific workflows, conventions, and best practices.

For detailed guidance on using Sherlock, see the ECHOLab Computing Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
GitHub serves as the primary entry point for ECHOLab research projects.

ECHOLab maintains a shared GitHub organization where lab repositories can be stored, maintained, and transferred across researchers over time. Storing projects within the ECHOLab organization helps improve discoverability, continuity, and collaboration while reducing the risk that project resources become inaccessible when researchers leave the lab.

The goal of a project repository is not necessarily to contain all project materials. Instead, repositories should help current and future collaborators understand the project, locate key resources, and identify where project work is taking place.

A new collaborator should be able to use a project's repository to quickly answer:

  • What is this project about?
  • Where are the data?
  • Where is the manuscript?
  • Where are project discussions occurring?
  • Who should I contact with questions?

docs.title: Recommended Repository Structure
docs.text:
ECHOLab maintains an ECHOLab project template to provide a lightweight starting point for new research projects.

The template provides a lightweight starting point for new projects and helps standardize project documentation across the lab.

Example:

project-name/

README.md

code/
├── 01_processing/
└── 02_analysis/

data/
└── README.md

Researchers may adapt this structure as needed for their projects.

docs.title: Project README
docs.text:
The README should serve as the primary project landing page.

Recommended sections include:

  • Project Overview
  • Project Description
  • Contributors
  • Project Resources
  • Contact

Example project resources:

  • Notion
  • Overleaf Drafts
  • Project Data Directory Location
  • Slack Channel
  • Presentations
  • Working Paper
  • Published Paper
  • Public Replication Repository

docs.title: GitHub and Other Tools
docs.text:
GitHub is one component of a broader project workflow.

Typical project resources may include:

Resource Purpose GitHub Project hub, documentation, and code Notion Project management and notes Overleaf Manuscript development Oak Shared data storage Sherlock High-performance computing

Different tools serve different purposes:

  • GitHub serves as the project's long-term entry point and source of institutional knowledge.
  • Notion supports active project management and day-to-day collaboration.
  • Overleaf supports manuscript development.
  • Oak stores shared datasets and large project files.
  • Sherlock provides computational resources for large analyses.

Because access to some Stanford-managed resources may change when researchers leave the lab or Stanford, project repositories should provide sufficient documentation and links that future collaborators can understand how a project was organized and where key resources are located.

docs.title: Repository Ownership
docs.text:
ECHOLab maintains a shared GitHub organization where lab repositories can be stored, maintained, and transferred across researchers over time.

Whenever possible, lab projects should be maintained within the ECHOLab GitHub organization rather than personal GitHub accounts. This helps improve discoverability, continuity, and collaboration while reducing the risk that project resources become inaccessible when researchers leave the lab.

The purpose of the ECHOLab GitHub organization is not to restrict ownership of research projects, but to provide a shared home for project resources and institutional knowledge.

Former lab members will retain access to the ECHOLab GitHub organization after leaving the lab. Continued access supports ongoing collaborations, manuscript revisions, and long-term maintenance of shared research products. Maintaining repositories within the organization helps ensure that projects remain accessible to both current researchers and lab alumni.

Personal repositories remain appropriate for exploratory work, software projects not associated with ECHOLab, and other individual efforts.

docs.title: Starting a New Project
docs.text:
Suggested workflow:

  1. Create a repository from the ECHOLab project template within the ECHOLab GitHub organization.
  2. Complete the project README.
  3. Create project-specific collaboration and project management resources as needed (e.g., Notion page, Slack channel, shared notes, etc.).
  4. Create an Overleaf project if applicable.
  5. Identify data storage locations.
  6. Link project resources from the repository.

docs.title: Replication Materials
docs.text:
Project repositories are intended to support active research.

When a project reaches publication, replication materials may be distributed through a separate public repository. Good project organization throughout the research process can substantially reduce the effort required to prepare replication materials.

docs.title: Shared Software and Code Resources
docs.text:
This page catalogs software, R packages, and reusable code resources developed by ECHOlab researchers that may be useful across projects.

Before building a new workflow from scratch, it is worth checking whether similar tools already exist.

docs.title: Heat
docs.text:
Repository: https://github.com/echolab-stanford/heat

Tools for processing gridded environmental datasets and generating exposure metrics for geographic units. The package was developed to support environmental epidemiology and climate-health analyses, but many functions are broadly applicable to spatial exposure assessment.

Potential applications:

  • Temperature exposures
  • Air pollution exposures
  • Population-weighted aggregation
  • Nonlinear exposure transformations
  • Aggregation of gridded datasets to polygons

docs.title: DHS.tools
docs.text:
Repository: https://github.com/echolab-stanford/DHS.tools

Tools for working with Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS) data. The package includes utilities for processing survey data and constructing commonly used measures.

Potential applications:

  • DHS data processing
  • Wealth index construction
  • Survey harmonization
  • Reproducible DHS workflows

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
If you have developed software, workflows, or reusable code that may be useful across projects, consider adding it to this page.

The goal is not to catalog every script, but to make broadly useful tools discoverable to future collaborators.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Submitting a paper is often less complicated than it first appears. While requirements vary by journal and field, most submissions follow a similar process.

This guide is written primarily from the perspective of environmental health, epidemiology, environmental science, and related fields, though many of the concepts apply more broadly.

docs.title: 1. Choose a Journal
docs.text:
Consider:

  • Scope and audience
  • Journal reputation and readership
  • Review timelines
  • Open access requirements
  • Publication costs

There is rarely a single correct choice. Journal selection often involves balancing audience, impact, speed, and cost.

docs.title: 2. Prepare Submission Materials
docs.text:
In addition to the manuscript, journals often require:

  • Cover letter
  • Supplementary materials
  • Data and code availability statements
  • Conflict of interest disclosures
  • Funding acknowledgments

Always review the journal's author guidelines before submission.

docs.title: 3. Write a Cover Letter
docs.text:
Most cover letters are short.

Typically include:

  • The manuscript title
  • The research question
  • Main findings
  • Why the paper fits the journal

In many fields, cover letters play a relatively minor role in editorial decisions.

docs.title: 4. Submit the Paper
docs.text:
Most journals use online submission systems.

Typical steps include:

  • Upload files
  • Enter author information
  • Complete disclosure forms
  • Suggest reviewers (if requested)

The process often takes longer than expected, particularly for large author teams.

docs.title: 5. What Happens Next?
docs.text:
After submission, manuscripts typically undergo:

  1. Editorial screening
  2. Peer review
  3. Editorial decision

Possible outcomes include:

  • Minor revision
  • Major revision
  • Reject and resubmit
  • Reject

Many journals reject a substantial fraction of papers before external review. A desk rejection is a normal part of the publication process and often reflects journal fit rather than a fundamental flaw in the science.

docs.title: 6. Publication Fees
docs.text:
Publication costs vary substantially across journals.

Common models include:

  • No publication fee
  • Optional open access fee
  • Mandatory open access fee

Fees are typically paid after acceptance, not at submission.

docs.title: 7. Working Papers and Preprints
docs.text:
Many researchers choose to make manuscripts publicly available before journal publication.

Potential benefits include:

  • Earlier dissemination of findings
  • Increased visibility and citations
  • Opportunities for feedback before publication
  • A public record of the work

Potential drawbacks include:

  • Public discussion of findings before peer review
  • Additional effort to update public versions
  • Concerns about being scooped in some fields

Norms vary across disciplines. In environmental health, environmental science, economics, and many related fields, public posting of working papers or preprints is common and often encouraged.

Examples include:

  • EarthArXiv
  • medRxiv
  • bioRxiv
  • SocArXiv
  • SSRN
  • NBER Working Papers

Researchers should verify that a target journal permits preprint posting, although many journals now do.

docs.title: 8. Responding to Reviews
docs.text:
When preparing a revision:

  • Respond to every comment.
  • Be respectful and professional.
  • Explain changes clearly.
  • Justify disagreements when necessary.

A detailed response document is often as important as the manuscript revisions themselves.

docs.title: Final Thought
docs.text: Most researchers learn the publication process through experience. When in doubt, consult the journal's author guidelines and ask colleagues who have submitted to the journal before.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Oak is ECHOLab's primary platform for shared data storage. Most shared datasets, project data, and lab-maintained resources should be stored on Oak whenever practical.

Researchers commonly interact with Oak in one of three ways:

  • Accessing data directly on Sherlock
  • Mounting Oak as a local filesystem using SSHFS
  • Synchronizing data between Oak and a local machine using rsync

This page provides guidance on common workflows used within ECHOLab.

Note: Oak resources are organized by PI. If you haven't already been added to Oak/mburke reach out to Sam.

docs.title: Oak is the Canonical Source of Truth
docs.text:
Researchers may maintain local copies of datasets for performance, convenience, or offline work.

However:

  • Shared datasets should live on Oak.
  • Oak should be treated as the canonical source of truth.
  • Local copies should be viewed as working copies.
  • Important updates should be synchronized back to Oak.

The existence of a file on a researcher's laptop should not be assumed to imply that it has been shared with the rest of the lab.

docs.title: Local Mirrors Are Not Automatically Synchronized
docs.text:
Synchronization between Oak and local machines is generally an explicit action.

This means:

  • New files created on Oak do not automatically appear on your laptop.
  • New files created on your laptop do not automatically appear on Oak.
  • Researchers are responsible for running synchronization commands when appropriate.

Always verify what will be transferred before performing large synchronization operations.

docs.title: Connecting to Oak
docs.text:
Many researchers find it convenient to mount Oak locally using SSHFS.

Example:

sshfs <SUNetID>@dtn.oak.stanford.edu:/oak/stanford/groups/<PI_SUNetID> ~/oak \
  -o cache=no \
  -o nolocalcaches \
  -o volname=oak-sshfs \
  -o defer_permissions

To unmount:

umount ~/oak

TODO: Verify and update connection commands as Oak infrastructure evolves.

docs.title: Avoiding Repeated Two-Factor Authentication
docs.text:
Researchers may configure SSH keys to reduce the need for repeated authentication.

Typical workflow:

  1. Generate an SSH key pair.
  2. Add the public key to Oak.
  3. Use the key for future connections.

TODO: Expand with current recommended Oak SSH key procedures.

Note that inactive keys may expire and require recreation.

docs.title: Finding Your Mounted Oak Drive
docs.text:
On macOS:

Cmd + Shift + G

Then navigate to:

~/oak

docs.title: Data Synchronization with rsync
docs.text:
Many ECHOLab researchers use rsync to synchronize data between Oak and local machines.

Common workflow:

docs.title: Preview Changes First
docs.text:
Use a "dry run" before transferring data.

Example:

rsync -avzn --progress source destination

The -n flag previews changes without actually transferring files.

Dry runs help prevent accidental overwrites and make it easier to understand what a synchronization command will do.

docs.title: Synchronizing Oak → Local
docs.text:
Typical use cases:

  • Creating a local working copy.
  • Updating local files from Oak.
  • Downloading newly added shared data.

Researchers should understand:

  • Files are copied only when synchronization is run.
  • Local files are generally not deleted automatically.
  • New Oak files do not appear locally until synchronization occurs.

docs.title: Synchronizing Local → Oak
docs.text:
Typical use cases:

  • Uploading processed data.
  • Sharing new files with collaborators.
  • Updating shared project resources.

Researchers should verify synchronization results before assuming files have been shared.

A file created locally remains local until explicitly synchronized to Oak.

docs.title: Oak Permissions
docs.text:
Shared Oak directories often use a permission structure that distinguishes between contributors and administrators.

Researchers may have permission to:

  • Read files
  • Create files
  • Modify files
  • Extend datasets

while lacking permission to:

  • Move directories
  • Rename directories
  • Reorganize shared folder structures
  • Modify access controls

This behavior is intentional and helps protect shared resources from accidental disruption.

If you receive a "Permission denied" error while moving or renaming shared directories, contact the dataset owner or directory administrator.

docs.title: Shared Data Governance
docs.text:
Researchers should coordinate major structural changes to shared datasets with the dataset owner or designated steward.

Examples include:

  • Renaming directories
  • Moving directories
  • Reorganizing shared folder structures
  • Archiving datasets
  • Modifying permissions

Contributors are generally encouraged to add data, update documentation, and extend datasets within established structures.

docs.title: Future Topics
docs.text:
The following topics should be expanded in future versions of this guide:

  • Detailed SSH key setup
  • Recommended rsync aliases
  • Sherlock ↔ Oak workflows
  • Common troubleshooting steps
  • Oak permissions and ACLs
  • Shared dataset stewardship
  • Local mirror best practices
  • Performance considerations for large datasets
